elements.sent-2411-051: This section discusses the key challenges involved in designing authentication mechanisms for legacy protocols.
elements.sent-2411-052: These challenges include traditional requirements - interoperability, minimal overhead, and low deployment costs - and newer demands - preserving data throughput, security parameter distribution, and compatibility with mixed systems.
elements.sent-2411-053: Together, these factors limit the adoption of state-of-the-art proposals in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-054: We then define specific security and operational design goals that stem from these challenges, along with the features that make ACRIC effective and practical.
elements.sent-2411-055: Figure \ref{fig:Layout visually illustrates the connections between these challenges, the derived design goals, and the features ACRIC offers.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.82\linewidth]{Figures/Layout.pdf} \caption{Relationship between (i) identified challenges in securing legacy systems and (ii) design goals to address them. From these goals, we derive key features for ACRIC.} \label{fig:Layout}
elements.sent-2411-056: In the past decade, substantial efforts have been dedicated to enhancing the security of legacy communication protocols, with a particular focus on industrial (\ac{ics and \ac{scada ) and in-vehicle networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: This focus derives from the rapid development of the \acrfull{iiot and automated driving technologies, which have increased the need for secure data exchange in traditionally isolated, now interconnected, environments~\cite{CybersecFor4.0, AddressingIndustry4.0, CybersecurityICS .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Much of this work concentrated on integrating security mechanisms such as \acp{ds , \acp{mac , encryption, and \acp{ids , into existing standard protocols and legacy devices~\cite{AnalysisSecurity, CyberattacksCountermeasures, CANSurvey, InvehicleAuth, SecurityIndustrial .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: However, despite theoretical progress, these solutions encounter significant challenges when applied in real-world, resource-constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-060: To better understand these limitations, we conducted an in-depth review of related works and collaborated directly with CAREL, a leading multinational in the \ac{ics sector for \ac{hvac systems — a domain marked by strict resource limitations.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: This process enabled us to identify key challenges that must be addressed when designing authentication solutions for legacy systems in resource-constrained environments~\cite{CANSurvey, SurveySecurity, SecurityChallenges, SecurityIndustrial, InvehicleAuth, S2-CAN, CANauth :.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Simultaneously upgrading all devices to a secure version is impractical and costly, especially in critical industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-064: Security solutions must ensure backward compatibility by preserving the original message frame structure.
elements.sent-2411-065: This challenge limits the extent of possible protocol modifications but is essential for enabling secure and non-secure devices to coexist and communicate seamlessly.
elements.sent-2411-067: Legacy protocols were originally designed to maximize data transmission efficiency, given the limited bandwidth and need for timely data exchange in their operational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-068: Adding authentication data and associated security parameters within the message payload reduces space available for actual data, thereby decreasing data throughput.
elements.sent-2411-069: This challenge requires designing security measures that do not compromise the data transmission rates critical to these environments.
elements.sent-2411-071: Secure communication typically relies on the distribution of cryptographic parameters.
elements.sent-2411-072: Most existing solutions treat key distribution as an external service, rather than integrating it into the protocol design.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, given the resource constraints and complexity of legacy environments, we argue that key distribution and initialization should be embedded within the protocol design process to ensure practicality in environments lacking external cryptographic service infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-075: Implementing security solutions that require additional hardware or substantial infrastructure modifications is prohibitively expensive, especially in industrial settings where large-scale device replacements are unfeasible.
elements.sent-2411-076: To encourage widespread adoption, security implementations must minimize deployment costs.
elements.sent-2411-078: Real-time performance is crucial in industrial and other time-sensitive applications.
elements.sent-2411-079: Ensuring that the security solution is lightweight and computationally efficient is essential to avoid negatively impacting the performance and reliability of critical systems.
elements.sent-2411-081: Existing solutions are tailored for specific communication protocols, requiring multiple implementations when different protocols are in use.
elements.sent-2411-082: This increases deployment costs and adds complexity.
elements.sent-2411-083: A single solution adaptable to diverse applications is highly preferable.
elements.sent-2411-084: Legacy systems are known to be vulnerable to a range of attacks, including message spoofing, modification, replay, \ac{mitm , and \ac{dos attacks~\cite{attack-taxonomies, Fovino_et_al, CANissues, SecurityChallenges .}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Unlike conventional IT security, which is centered on the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) triad, systems such as \ac{ics and in-vehicle networks prioritize the Availability, Integrity, and Confidentiality (AIC) model~\cite{ICS_security, SecurityChallenges .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: This focus reflects the importance of continuous availability and data integrity, as disruptions or alterations to data can significantly impact physical operations and safety~\cite{Securing_SCADA .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Confidentiality, on the other hand, is often deprioritized for several reasons~\cite{Wright-et-al, Castellanos-et-al, Cherifi-et-al : (i) the limited computational capacity of these devices constrains their ability to execute complex encryption operations; (ii) encryption introduces latency, which is problematic in real-time control systems where rapid response times are essential; (iii) communications are often predictable due to their ties to physical processes, allowing attackers to infer encrypted content without necessarily decrypting it.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Consequently, the primary security objectives for these systems focus on ensuring~\cite{Fovino_et_al, SecurityChallenges, AnalysisSecurity :.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Authentication: Ensures that all entities involved in communication are verified and legitimate.
elements.sent-2411-090: Integrity Protection: Prevents unauthorized modifications to messages, thereby preserving the accuracy and trustworthiness of the data.
elements.sent-2411-091: Replay Protection: Prevents the reuse of previously valid transmitted data, reducing the risk of unauthorized actions.
elements.sent-2411-092: Operational goals address the previously discussed challenges, making ACRIC practical and ready for real-world adoption.
elements.sent-2411-093: To this end, we aim to ensure:.
elements.sent-2411-095: The solution must integrate with the existing infrastructure, allowing secure and non-secure devices to coexist on the same network.
elements.sent-2411-096: Maintaining the protocol's original frame format enables fitting into existing systems without modifications.
elements.sent-2411-097: Additionally, the solution must avoid extensive changes or the introduction of additional hardware.
elements.sent-2411-098: ACRIC meets this goal by leveraging the \ac{crc field, present in most legacy communication protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-099: This approach is cost-effective, requiring only software updates without adding new hardware.
elements.sent-2411-101: The solution must maintain data throughput by utilizing existing protocol structures, avoiding the need for additional payload space.
elements.sent-2411-102: ACRIC achieves this using the existing \ac{crc field, thereby preserving payload size and data throughput.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The solution must include a built-in, efficient initialization process for key agreements and updates.
elements.sent-2411-105: ACRIC includes an efficient key agreement and distribution procedure by design, ensuring the system remains secure and easy to maintain.
elements.sent-2411-107: The solution must support real-time operations, a common requirement in legacy systems.
elements.sent-2411-108: ACRIC is optimized to minimize computational load and communication delays, avoiding resource-intensive cryptographic operations in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-110: The solution must be suitable for a variety of communication protocols.
elements.sent-2411-111: ACRIC utilizes the \ac{crc field, which is present in most legacy protocols, thus being applicable across various systems.}
elements.sent-2411-112: By achieving these design goals, ACRIC addresses the identified challenges, providing a robust security solution tailored to the specific needs and constraints of legacy communication environments.
elements.sent-2411-113: This section provides the essential background information to fully comprehend the ACRIC framework.
elements.sent-2411-114: Section~\ref{subsec:CRC introduces the concept of \ac{crc , emphasizing its main features and parameters; Section~\ref{sec:Crypto discusses cryptographic hash functions and hash chains, key components of our authentication mechanism.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: In \ac{crc computation, data is represented as a binary polynomial and is divided by a generator polynomial of degree \(n\) using modulo two arithmetic~\cite{CRC .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: This simplifies operations to XOR without carries.
elements.sent-2411-117: This \(n\)-bit remainder of this division forms the \ac{crc value.}
elements.sent-2411-118: During transmission, the receiver recalculates the \ac{crc : a zero remainder indicates error-free data, while a non-zero remainder signals an error.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The choice of generator polynomial - an \(n\)-degree polynomial with binary coefficients - is critical to \ac{crc ’s effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Irreducible polynomials ensure detection of any single error within a certain block length and maximize error-detection capabilities by maintaining a high minimum Hamming distance between valid codes~\cite{CRC2 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, a well-chosen generator can detect all burst errors up to a specific length and a high percentage of longer bursts, enhancing data reliability.
elements.sent-2411-122: Thus, generator polynomial selection balances computational efficiency with error-detection performance~\cite{CRC, CRC2 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: Variations in \ac{iv value, which sets the \ac{crc ’s starting state, as well as input/output data reflection and final XOR values, can modify the \ac{crc calculation but do not impact its error-detection capability, which depends primarily on the generator polynomial~\cite{CRC2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The simplicity of bitwise operations makes \acp{crc ideal for hardware implementation and real-time applications.}
elements.sent-2411-125: A hash function is a mathematical function \(H\left(\cdot\right)\) that takes an input of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-length binary string \(h\), known as the hash value or digest.
elements.sent-2411-126: Ideally, the output is unique to each input data and should be uniformly distributed across the output space~\cite{hash-review .. Formally: \begin{equation}H := \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} = \mathbb{B}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{B}^m \,, \, \text{where } \mathbb{B} = \left\{0,1\right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}H := X \rightarrow Y = B^n \rightarrow B^m \,, \, where  B = \left\{0,1\right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-127: Cryptographic hash functions are specialized hash functions that meet specific security properties, making them suitable for cryptographic applications~\cite{fips-hmac .}
elements.sent-2411-128: They must satisfy the following properties~\cite{hash-review, on-SHA :.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Pre-Image Resistance: Given \(y \in \mathcal{Y}\), it must be computationally infeasible to find any \(x \in \mathcal{X}\) such that \(H\left(x\right) = y\).
elements.sent-2411-130: Second Pre-image Resistance: Given \(x_1 \in \mathcal{X}\), it must be computationally infeasible to find \(x_2 \in \mathcal{X \,,\, x_2 \neq x_1\), such that \(H\left(x_1\right) = H\left(x_2\right)\).}
elements.sent-2411-131: Collision Resistance: It must be computationally infeasible to find any two distinct inputs \(\left(x_1, x_2\right) \in \mathcal{X ^2 \,,\, x_1 \neq x_2\), such that \(H\left(x_1\right) = H\left(x_2\right)\).}
elements.sent-2411-132: Avalanche Effect: A small change in the input should result in a significantly different hash value, reflecting a high sensitivity to input variations.
elements.sent-2411-133: A hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\) is created by repeatedly applying a hash function, using each output as the input for the next step.
elements.sent-2411-134: This process creates a sequence of $n$ hash values \(\mathcal{H = \left\{h_0, h_1, \dots, h_n\right\}\)~\cite{hash_chains .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The relationship between these values is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ChainDefinition} \mathcal{H} = \begin{cases} h_0 = H\left(x\right) h_i = H\left(h_{i-1}\right) \text{ for } i = 1, \dots, n. \end{cases}\end{equation} This cryptographic tool is often used for authentication, relying on the pre-image resistance property of the adopted hash function.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ChainDefinition} H =  h_0 = H\left(x\right) h_i = H\left(h_{i-1}\right)  for  i = 1, \dots, n. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-136: Cryptographic mechanisms that provide integrity and authenticity using a cryptographic hash function in combination with a secret key are termed \acp{hmac ~\cite{fips-hmac .}}

elements.sent-2411-148: To mitigate this, we assume that group memberships are strictly managed, with devices assigned only the permissions necessary for their roles to reduce unauthorized access and prevent masquerade attacks.

elements.sent-2411-152: Our threat model draws on frameworks used in prior works~\cite{CANSurvey, CybersecCAN, S2-CAN, RetrofittingIntegrity, Katulic-et-al, Bernieri-et-al and aligns with the established Dolev-Yao model~\cite{DV_model , which comprehensively defines attacker capabilities in both networked and cyber-physical environments.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: By leveraging these models, we adopt widely accepted assumptions and principles to outline potential communication threats.. Attacker's Capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-154: We assume a well-resourced attacker with substantial network access capabilities, balanced by some specific constraints:.
elements.sent-2411-155: Access to the Network: The attacker has complete access to the communication network, enabling interception, modification, and injection of messages.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, the attacker cannot compromise hardware or internal cryptographic mechanisms, consistent with the Dolev-Yao model~\cite{DV_model .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Act as passive and active roles: The attacker can passively eavesdrop on sensitive information and actively inject, modify, or replay messages.
elements.sent-2411-158: Knowledge of Protection Mechanisms: The attacker is aware of the authentication mechanism in use.
elements.sent-2411-159: This aligns with Kerckhoff's principle, which states that system security should depend on the secrecy of the keys, not the protocol itself~\cite{Kerckhoff_principle .}
elements.sent-2411-161: In line with previous studies, the attacker may pursue the following objectives:.

elements.sent-2411-170: ACRIC enhances the security of legacy protocols by providing message integrity protection and authentication.
elements.sent-2411-171: Legacy communication protocols typically include a \ac{crc field to detect transmission errors, calculated using an \acrfull{iv , a generator polynomial, and the message content.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: While standard \ac{crc serves as a basic integrity check, it does not offer integrity protection.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Hence, because protocols often standardize the \ac{iv and generator polynomial, an attacker can alter the message payload, compute the corresponding \ac{crc , and replace the original \ac{crc to evade detection.}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: ACRIC repurposes the \ac{crc field to provide message integrity and authenticity.}
elements.sent-2411-175: This is achieved by incorporating a secret \ac{iv and applying \ac{otp encryption.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: The proposed methodology consists of three phases:.
elements.sent-2411-177: Initialization: This is the procedure for setting up or refreshing cryptographic parameters for authenticated communications.
elements.sent-2411-178: Specifically, this phases enables the involved parties to establish a secret session key and a hash chain initialization value.
elements.sent-2411-179: This secret \ac{iv verifies both the integrity and authenticity of the message.}
elements.sent-2411-180: A matching between calculated and received \ac{crc indicates the message is legitimate, as only authorized parties possess the correct \ac{iv .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: To counter this risk, ACRIC encrypts the \ac{crc before transmission using \ac{otp encryption — applying an XOR operation between the \ac{crc and a single-use secret key shared between communicating parties.}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: This one-time use of the key prevents attackers from reverse-engineering the initialization sequence.
elements.sent-2411-183: By introducing a secret \ac{iv and encrypting the \ac{crc , ACRIC significantly strengthens the security of legacy communication systems, ensuring both integrity protection and authentication while maintaining compatibility with standard protocols and non-secured devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Detailed discussions on key management and cryptographic considerations are provided in subsequent sections.
elements.sent-2411-185: To address deployment cost constraints and simplify implementation, our approach assumes that each device in the network is equipped with a long-term master key, \(K_m\).
elements.sent-2411-186: This design choice reduces the complexity and expense of deploying the security mechanism, aiming to streamline the deployment phase.
elements.sent-2411-187: Adding static security parameters, such as counters, would increase deployment complexity by requiring precise synchronization across devices and groups.
elements.sent-2411-188: Instead, our approach generates supplementary security parameters dynamically at runtime, minimizing the need for pre-loaded, synchronized values and reducing human intervention.
elements.sent-2411-189: Nevertheless, for completeness, we also consider the option of pre-loading additional security parameters during installation, leaving the design choice to the manufacturers' convenience.
elements.sent-2411-190: Furthermore, ACRIC avoids relying on identity-based authentication, meaning that identity information is not used in computing security values.
elements.sent-2411-191: This design choice accommodates protocols that do not inherently use unique node identifiers, such as the CAN-bus protocol~\cite{CAN .}
elements.sent-2411-192: The initialization procedure distributes essential security parameters for secure communication, specifically establishing symmetric session keys \(K_s\) and shared hash chains \(\mathcal{H}\) among participants.
elements.sent-2411-193: This procedure occurs during system installation and periodically to refresh security parameters.
elements.sent-2411-194: We identify two main approaches for initializing the system and establishing secure session keys: static computation and dynamic computation, each with distinct advantages and limitations regarding security, complexity, and practicality.
elements.sent-2411-195: In the static approach, entities compute the session key independently, without requiring message exchanges, deriving it directly from the master key.
elements.sent-2411-196: For unique session keys within each communication group or pair, a shared secret value C must be distributed exclusively among relevant entities.
elements.sent-2411-197: In \ac{p2p communications, C acts as a pair-based secret, while in broadcast scenarios, it serves as a group-based secret.}
elements.sent-2411-198: A major advantage of this approach is that key agreement is achieved locally, eliminating the need for message exchanges and reducing runtime communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-199: However, it adds complexity to deployment, as each device must be carefully pre-configured with the necessary secret values C$_i$.
elements.sent-2411-200: This manual configuration increases the risk of human error, which could compromise network security or cause initialization failures.
elements.sent-2411-201: The dynamic approach, by contrast, involves active interactions between entities to establish the session key.
elements.sent-2411-202: Here, directly deriving the session key from the master key, as in the static approach, is impractical because a unique shared secret value C must be generated dynamically.
elements.sent-2411-203: Generating C dynamically introduces challenges: (i) if one node generates C and then distributes it, confidentiality must be ensured.
elements.sent-2411-204: Encrypting with the master key \(K_m\) is insecure, as all nodes have access to \(K_m\) and could decrypt it retrieving C.
elements.sent-2411-205: Additionally, (ii) pre-loading C would revert the system to the static model, negating the purpose of a dynamic approach.
elements.sent-2411-206: To overcome these challenges, we must employ a key agreement protocol, such as Diffie-Hellman~\cite{DiffieHellman , which securely establishes the session key through authenticated exchanges.}
elements.sent-2411-207: The dynamic approach offers significant deployment simplicity by requiring only a common master key for initial configuration, with other parameters generated at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-208: This reduces the need for manually configuring individual secret values.
elements.sent-2411-209: However, dynamic initialization requires active communication among entities, which increases initialization time due to multiple protocol rounds for secure session key establishment.
elements.sent-2411-210: Both static and dynamic approaches are viable, with the choice depending on network requirements, security constraints, and deployment context.
elements.sent-2411-211: Table~\ref{tab:InitApproach summarizes the advantages and disadvantages of each approach discussed above.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Given the simplicity of static computation, we present a potential solution for dynamic initialization using an authenticated Diffie-Hellman protocol, balancing security with practical deployment considerations.

elements.sent-2411-213: Model Secured network nodes are equipped with a shared master key \(K_m\).
elements.sent-2411-214: For each intended receiver, the transmitter initiates an authenticated Diffie-Hellman exchange to securely establish a session key \(K_s\) and a hash chain initialization value \(l\).. We define:.
elements.sent-2411-215: Tx: the device initiating the communication;.
elements.sent-2411-216: Rx: the device receiving the communication.
elements.sent-2411-217: The initialization procedure follows these steps:.
elements.sent-2411-218: Key Generation \(\left(a, A\right)\): Tx samples a random private key \(a\) and computes the corresponding public key \(A = g^a \, mod \, p\).
elements.sent-2411-219: DH\_msg1: Tx sends \(A\) to Rx, authenticated with \(h^*_0 = H\left(K_m, A\right)\).
elements.sent-2411-221: Verification of \(\left(h^*_0\right)\): Rx verifies the authenticity of the received message \(\left(\left[\Tilde{A || \Tilde{h ^*_0\right]\right)\) by checking if \(\hat{h ^*_0 = H\left(K_m, \Tilde{A}\right) \overset{? {= \Tilde{h ^*_0\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Key Generation \(\left(b, B\right)\): Upon successful verification, Rx samples a random private key \(b\) and computes its public key \(B = g^b \, mod \, p\).
elements.sent-2411-223: DH\_msg2: Rx sends \(B\) to Tx, authenticated with \(h^*_1 = H\left(K_m, B\right)\).
elements.sent-2411-225: Verification of \(\left(h^*_1\right)\): Tx verifies the authenticity of the received message, confirming \(\hat{h ^*_1 = H\left(K_m, \Tilde{B}\right) \overset{? {= \Tilde{h ^*_1\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Upon successful verification, Tx computes the shared session key \(K_s = B^a \, mod \, p = \left(g^b\right)^a \, mod \, p\).
elements.sent-2411-227: HC\_msg1: Tx generates a random initialization value \(l\) for the hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\).
elements.sent-2411-228: Tx sends \(l\), encrypted and authenticated as: \(HC\_msg1 = \left[l \oplus K_s \, || \, H\left(K_s, l\right)\right] = \left[L \, || \, h^*_2\right]\).
elements.sent-2411-229: Verification of \(\left(h^*_2\right)\): Rx computes \(K_s = A^b \, mod \, p\) and retrieves \(\hat{l = \Tilde{L \oplus K_s = \left(\Tilde{l \oplus K_s}\right) \oplus K_s\) It then verifies the message by checking if \(\hat{h ^*_2 = H\left(K_s, \hat{l}\right) \overset{? {= \Tilde{h ^*_2\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: HC\_msg2: If verification succeeds, Rx responds with \(HC\_msg2 = \left[H\left(K_s, l+1\right)\right] = \left[h^*_3\right]\), confirming the correct receipt of \(l\).
elements.sent-2411-231: Verification \(\left(h^*_3\right)\): Tx checks for the authenticity of the response.
elements.sent-2411-232: If successful, both Tx and Rx compute the hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\).
elements.sent-2411-233: If unsuccessful, Tx notifies Rx of the error, requiring a reinitialization.
elements.sent-2411-234: Upon successful initialization, Tx and Rx securely share the session key \(K_s\) and the hash chain initialization value \(l\): \begin{equation}\label{eq:ChainCompute} \mathcal{H} = \begin{cases} h_0 = H\left(K_s, l\right), h_i = H\left(K_s, h_{i-1}\right) \; \text{for} \; i = 1, \dots, N. \end{cases}\end{equation} Elements from this hash chain will authenticate future messages.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ChainCompute} H =  h_0 = H\left(K_s, l\right), h_i = H\left(K_s, h_{i-1}\right) \; for \; i = 1, \dots, N. \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-236: To enable secure communication within a group of entities, we employ a Group Diffie-Hellman protocol to establish a shared session key among all group members.
elements.sent-2411-237: This protocol extends the traditional \ac{p2p Diffie-Hellman approach, allowing secure key agreement across multiple nodes and ensuring that each participating entity derives the same session key.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In a Group Diffie-Hellman setup, each node generates its own public-private key pair and shares its public key with every other group member.
elements.sent-2411-239: This initial exchange ensures all nodes have the necessary public keys to collaboratively compute a shared session key.
elements.sent-2411-240: After the public key exchange, each node independently computes the session key using sequential multiplication and exponentiation.
elements.sent-2411-241: Specifically, each node collects the public keys of the other group members, multiplies these keys together, and raises the product to the power of its own private key.
elements.sent-2411-242: This process results in a single session key consistently derived by all nodes, ensuring secure and uniform key establishment across the group.
elements.sent-2411-243: Following this, a designated group member generates the initialization value \(l\) for the hash chain and securely shares it with the group, similar to the \ac{p2p case.}
elements.sent-2411-244: The Group Diffie-Hellman protocol provides a scalable and efficient solution for secure group communication, enabling synchronized key generation across multiple devices without requiring individual key agreements for each pairwise connection.
elements.sent-2411-245: This approach simplifies key management and enhances security by minimizing the number of key exchange interactions needed in large groups.
elements.sent-2411-246: Upon successful initialization, each node has the security parameters required to authenticate messages: (i) a master key \(K_m\), (ii) one or more session keys \(K_s\), and (iii) one or more hash chains \(\mathcal{H}\).
elements.sent-2411-247: A primary objective of ACRIC is to minimize runtime computational and transmission overhead, particularly during message processing, while still providing robust authentication and integrity protection.
elements.sent-2411-248: To accomplish this, ACRIC adds only a single \ac{otp encryption to the standard protocol’s runtime operations, implemented as a lightweight XOR with minimal overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-249: By repurposing the \ac{crc field for message integrity protection and authentication, ACRIC ensures full backward compatibility with existing protocols and supports both broadcast and \ac{p2p communication models.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: The authentication process is consistent across these scenarios, with the only exception being \ac{p2p protocols allowing broadcast messaging.}
elements.sent-2411-251: For instance, the client-server protocol \ac{modbus enables clients to send broadcast messages~\cite{modbus-application .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: This case is addressed separately in Section~\ref{subsec:P2PBroadcast .}
elements.sent-2411-253: A core concept in ACRIC is replacing the default \ac{iv in \ac{crc computation with a secret value known only to the involved parties.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: We propose using a truncated version of \(K_s\).
elements.sent-2411-255: Given \(K_s = \left[k^s_0 || k^s_1 || \cdots || k^s_n\right]\), where each \(k^s_i\) is an 8-bit binary sequence, we define \(\ac{iv = \left[k^s_0 || k^s_1\right]\).}
elements.sent-2411-256: However, using a secret \ac{iv alone does not provide sufficient security.}
elements.sent-2411-257: An attacker could potentially deduce the \ac{iv by analyzing the \ac{crc and the associated message payload.}}
elements.sent-2411-258: By brute-forcing all possible binary sequences for the \ac{iv , an attacker will find a match, allowing them to compute the \ac{crc of a forged message that the receiver would accept.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: To prevent this, ACRIC protects the \ac{crc by encrypting it with \ac{otp using a value from the hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\) as the encryption key.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Thanks to the perfect secrecy property of \ac{otp ~\cite{OTP , this encryption prevents attackers from gaining any advantage through analysis of the encrypted \ac{crc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: Let \(F_{CRC}\left(I, M\right)\) represent the standard \ac{crc algorithm, which uses the initialization vector \(I\) and message \(M\) as inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-262: In ACRIC, the secure \ac{crc computation for authentication and integrity, termed \(SecCRC\), for a given message \(M_i\), is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:authentication} SecCRC_i \left(M_i\right) = CRC_i \oplus h_i = F_{CRC}\left(\ac{iv}, M_i\right) \oplus h_i,\end{equation} where \(i\) denotes the message index, and \(h_i \in \mathcal{H}\) is the \(i\)-th hash value.}

elements.sent-2411-278: In the ACRIC framework, hash values - and by extension, hash chains - are fundamental to security.
elements.sent-2411-279: These values serve as secret keys for \ac{otp encryption, securing communications by preventing attackers from recovering the \ac{iv needed to compute the \ac{crc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Managing these chains can be challenging for memory-constrained devices, particularly in large networks where multiple chains must be maintained.
elements.sent-2411-281: Efficient hash chain management is therefore essential, as it impacts computational and memory resources, balancing security requirements with available resources.
elements.sent-2411-282: This section explores strategies for managing hash chains, examining their constraints, requirements, benefits, and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-283: This analysis provides a basis for choosing the most appropriate strategy based on the application context, communication model, protocol requirements, and specific constraints.
elements.sent-2411-284: Tables~\ref{tab:HashChain and~\ref{tab:HashChainComputation summarize the advantages and drawbacks of various approaches to hash chain consumption, storage, and computation.}}
elements.sent-2411-285: Hash chain consumption refers to the methods for utilizing individual hash values within a chain.
elements.sent-2411-286: Based on the hash chain defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ChainDefinition we identify two primary consumption strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-287: The first strategy is backward consumption, where hash values are used in reverse order, starting from the tail \(h_n\) and progressing back to the root \(h_0\).
elements.sent-2411-288: Due to the pre-image resistance property of hash functions (see Section~\ref{sec:Crypto ), even if a particular hash \(h_i\) is exposed, it remains computationally infeasible to derive any prior values \(h_j\) for \(j < i\).}
elements.sent-2411-289: However, this approach requires the entire chain to be pre-computed before any values can be used, increasing memory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-290: The second strategy is forward consumption, where hash values are consumed sequentially from the root \(h_0\) toward the tail \(h_n\).
elements.sent-2411-291: This approach eliminates the need for pre-computing the entire chain, allowing each value to be calculated in real time, thus reducing memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-292: However, forward consumption requires the inclusion of a secret key to ensure forward secrecy; without it, an attacker with access to \(h_i\) could compute all subsequent values \(h_j \;, \forall \, j > i+1\), posing a security risk.
elements.sent-2411-293: By incorporating a secret key in the hashing process, this risk is mitigated, as an attacker would be unable to compute subsequent hash values without full access to the hash function input.
elements.sent-2411-294: We identify two main storage strategies that define how hash chains are managed across communicating devices.
elements.sent-2411-295: The first strategy is entity-based hash chain storage, where each device maintains its own chain for authenticating its transmitted messages.
elements.sent-2411-296: This approach presents two key limitations: (i) it may require defining device identities, which some protocols (e.g. CAN-bus protocol~\cite{CAN ) do not inherently support; and (ii) receiving devices must keep track of the index of each used hash value for accurate verification.}
elements.sent-2411-297: To manage this, either all devices must monitor transmissions from the source, or an additional index parameter must be included in the payload to synchronize receivers with the sender’s current hash value.
elements.sent-2411-298: However, the latter solution reduces data throughput as such index has to be added to the message payload.. Memory analysis.
elements.sent-2411-299: Let \(N\) be the number of network nodes or, in a broadcast scenario, the number of group members.
elements.sent-2411-300: Memory requirements for each device are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-301: Backward consumption: Each device must store a full hash chain for itself and \(N-1\) individual hash values for other nodes.
elements.sent-2411-302: Forward consumption: Each device stores a total of \(N\) hash values, one for its own chain and \(N-1\) for other nodes.
elements.sent-2411-303: The second strategy is pair-based hash chain storage, where each pair of nodes, is assigned a unique hash chain.
elements.sent-2411-304: For broadcast communications using group keys, this becomes group-based hash chain storage, where the group collectively uses a single shared chain.
elements.sent-2411-305: Unlike the entity-based approach, this method eliminates the need for synchronization between sender and receiver.. Memory analysis.
elements.sent-2411-306: With \(N\) representing the number of network nodes or groups a node belongs to, memory requirements are:.
elements.sent-2411-307: Backward consumption: Each node stores \(N-1\) full hash chains for \ac{p2p communication, or \(N\) full hash chains in a broadcast setup.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Forward consumption: Each node stores \(N-1\) individual hash values for \ac{p2p communications, or \(N\) single hash values in a broadcast model.}
elements.sent-2411-309: Table~\ref{tab:HashChain shows that the forward-consume pair-based approach requires the least storage overhead, making it particularly suitable for legacy and resource-constrained devices.}

elements.sent-2411-310: The hash chain computation strategy plays a crucial role in determining system performance and security.
elements.sent-2411-311: We identify three main strategies: runtime, offline, and hybrid.
elements.sent-2411-312: Table~\ref{tab:HashChainComputation compares the advantages and disadvantages of each strategy and indicates which chain consumption methods are compatible with each approach.}
elements.sent-2411-313: In the runtime computation strategy, hash values are generated as needed during operation, ensuring freshness and reducing susceptibility to pre-computation attacks.
elements.sent-2411-314: This approach minimizes memory requirements, as only the current hash needs to be stored.
elements.sent-2411-315: However, it increases computational demands and may introduce communication latency due to the real-time cryptographic calculations required.
elements.sent-2411-316: The offline computation strategy involves pre-computing the entire hash chain before deployment, which minimizes real-time processing demands but requires significant memory to store the full chain.
elements.sent-2411-317: Finally, the hybrid strategy combines elements of both runtime and offline computation by pre-computing and storing part of the chain, with the remainder calculated as needed.
elements.sent-2411-318: This approach balances the reduced runtime demands of offline computation with lower memory requirements, achieving a compromise between system efficiency and security.

elements.sent-2411-319: In this section, we evaluate ACRIC.
elements.sent-2411-320: Section~\ref{subsec:Security assesses ACRIC's security guarantees against the security goals previously defined.}
elements.sent-2411-321: Section~\ref{subsec:Performance evaluates ACRIC's performance in terms of computational and transmission overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-322: We first theoretically discuss ACRIC's security guarantees, then we present formal verification and brute-force resistance assessment to sustain our findings.
elements.sent-2411-323: The proposed initialization procedure relies on three key elements: (i) cryptographic hash functions, (ii) the Diffie-Hellman protocol, and (iii) \ac{otp encryption.}
elements.sent-2411-324: The overall security of the procedure is therefore determined by the weakest of these elements.
elements.sent-2411-325: The cryptographic hash function ensures that it is computationally infeasible to predict \(h = H\left(K, A\right)\) without knowing the key \(K\), or to retrieve \(K\) from observing \(A\) and \(h\) (see Section~\ref{sec:Crypto ).}
elements.sent-2411-326: Consequently, as long as the master key \(K_m\) remains confidential, the hash values preserve their security, ensuring message authenticity.
elements.sent-2411-327: The security of the Diffie-Hellman protocol is based on the difficulty of solving the discrete logarithm problem in polynomial time~\cite{DiffieHellman .}
elements.sent-2411-328: However, it is well-known that it is susceptible to \ac{mitm attacks if carried out without authentication~\cite{DH-MITM .}}
elements.sent-2411-329: To address this vulnerability, we incorporate hash values to authenticate exchanged messages.
elements.sent-2411-330: Since we already established the security of the hash values, we conclude that the Diffie-Hellman key agreement defined as in Section~\ref{subsec:Initialization is secure, and that the session key \(K_s\) remains confidential.}
elements.sent-2411-331: As a result, the confidentiality of \(l\) is is assured through \ac{otp encryption, benefiting from perfect secrecy.}
elements.sent-2411-332: It is essential to note that the secrecy of \(l\) relies on \(K_s\) never being reused as \ac{otp key in future communications, a condition that is strictly maintained.}
elements.sent-2411-333: We formally verified ACRIC's security properties using the Scyther tool\footnote{\url{https://people.cispa.io/cas.cremers/scyther/ , which is designed for the automated formal verification of security protocols.}}
elements.sent-2411-334: Scyther assesses the confidentiality and integrity of protocols by modeling adversarial capabilities and systematically verifying the protocol's resilience against potential attacks.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our verification focused on evaluating the confidentiality of security parameters associated with initialization against external attackers.
elements.sent-2411-336: The results confirm that ACRIC's design preserves the secrecy of underlying parameters (see Appendix~\ref{sec:FormalVerification for further details).}
elements.sent-2411-337: For transparency and reproducibility, the source code used in this formal verification is publicly available in a GitHub repository\footnote{\url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ACRIC-7B70/README.md (anonymized for submission).}}
elements.sent-2411-338: By using a secret \ac{iv for \ac{crc computation, followed by \ac{otp encryption with hash values derived from a secure hash chain, ACRIC provides message integrity protection and authentication.}}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Ensuring the authentication property means that any message authenticated with an incorrect \ac{iv or hash value will be rejected.}
elements.sent-2411-340: Thus, for a given message \(M\), we calculate the collision probability of obtaining the correct \(SecCRC\) with incorrect security parameters.
elements.sent-2411-342: Case 1: Correct \ac{iv and incorrect hash value .}
elements.sent-2411-343: The probability of achieving a \(SecCRC\) collision is zero.
elements.sent-2411-344: With the correct \ac{iv , the correct \ac{crc is computed.}}
elements.sent-2411-345: However, applying XOR with an incorrect hash value cannot yield the correct \(SecCRC\).

elements.sent-2411-393: In this section, we discuss how ACRIC addresses and meets the identified challenges and design goals, respectively, demonstrating its suitability for real-world adoption.
elements.sent-2411-394: Regarding the security goals — authentication, integrity protection, and replay resistance — Section~\ref{subsec:Security demonstrates that ACRIC fully meets these objectives.}

elements.sent-2411-396: For the operational goals, ACRIC effectively overcomes the identified limitations and challenges (SectionSection~\ref{sec:Challenges ).}
elements.sent-2411-397: Specifically, we designed an efficient key agreement procedure compatible with both \ac{p2p and broadcast communication models, minimizing deployment complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-398: Additionally, by repurposing the \ac{crc field to provide security with only a software update, we preserved data throughput and backward compatibility, reducing deployment costs and enabling interoperability between secure and non-secure devices.}
elements.sent-2411-399: Finally, experimental results from real-world tests show that ACRIC is lightweight and preserves the system’s real-time responsiveness, ensuring that security enhancements do not compromise operational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-400: In conclusion, the proposed authentication mechanism meets all defined design goals, addressing security and operational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-401: It enhances the protection of legacy systems by ensuring authentication, integrity, and replay protection while maintaining real-time response and backward compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-402: This comprehensive approach makes ACRIC a practical and secure solution, ready for adoption in industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-403: Designing authentication mechanisms for legacy systems in resource-constrained environments presents several challenges that hinder the effective adoption of contemporary security solutions in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-404: Legacy systems are often characterized by limited processing power, constrained memory resources, and adherence to predefined communication protocols that lack inherent security features.
elements.sent-2411-405: Implementing robust authentication without compromising system performance or interoperability is a significant challenge in such environments.
elements.sent-2411-406: Several proposals in the literature involve using asymmetric encryption, digital signatures, digital certificates, or the integration of protocols like Transport Layer Security (TLS) to provide security and authenticity~\cite{Ferst-et-al, modbus-security, Rao-et-al, Shahzad-et-al, authentiCAN, ADSB-2, ADSB-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-407: While these methods offer strong security guarantees, they are unsuitable for resource-constrained environments as they rely on computationally intensive cryptographic operations.
elements.sent-2411-408: This often results in considerable transmission delays and significant memory and computational overhead, which resource-constrained systems cannot support.
elements.sent-2411-409: To address these limitations, many researchers have turned to \acp{mac , particularly \acp{hmac ~\cite{Hayes_et_al, Liu-et-al, Pricop-et-al, Stancu-et-al, Xuan-et-al, Yi-et-al, vatiCAN, Libracan, VulCAN, ADSB-1, lightweightMAC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-410: Hash functions are less computation-intensive than asymmetric cryptographic algorithms and can be efficiently implemented even on devices with limited resources.
elements.sent-2411-411: However, solutions adopting this approach often face significant limitations.
elements.sent-2411-412: Typically, the \ac{mac value is appended to the original message, altering the standard frame format defined by the legacy protocol.}
elements.sent-2411-413: This disrupts backward compatibility and interoperability since non-secured may not recognize or properly handle the modified frames.
elements.sent-2411-414: Alternatively, if the \ac{mac is included within the payload, it is often associated with security parameters such as counters, nonces, or timestamps.}
elements.sent-2411-415: This reduces the amount of application data that can be transmitted, thereby decreasing data throughput.
elements.sent-2411-416: Since high data throughput is often a critical requirement that influenced the original design and adoption of legacy protocols, any reduction can adversely affect system performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-417: Another prevalent strategy is the \ac{bitw solution, wherein cryptographic operations are offloaded to external devices placed in front of legacy systems~\cite{Fovino_et_al, Katulic-et-al, Lin-et-al, Rajesh-et-al, Tidrea-et-al, Tsang-et-al, Wright-et-al,lightweightMAC .}}
elements.sent-2411-418: These devices act as security gateways, encrypting and authenticating communications on behalf of the legacy system.
elements.sent-2411-419: While \ac{bitw solutions mitigate the resource limitations of legacy devices, they introduce additional hardware into the system architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-420: This increases deployment complexity, raises costs, and potentially introduces new points of failure.
elements.sent-2411-421: Using covert channels to carry authentication information represents another alternative~\cite{Bernieri-et-al, TACAN .}
elements.sent-2411-422: This approach avoids modifying the original packet format, facilitating interoperability and maintaining compatibility with unsecured devices.
elements.sent-2411-423: However, implementing covert channels often necessitates additional hardware or modifications to existing hardware to support the embedding and extracting of hidden data.
elements.sent-2411-424: A recently emerging approach involves enhancing the \ac{crc field, traditionally used for error detection, to provide authentication and integrity protection by computing it cryptographically.}
elements.sent-2411-425: Solutions following this strategy aim to secure the CRC computation by making the generator polynomial secret~\cite{CRC2, CRC3, CRC4, CRC5, CRC6, CRC7 .}
elements.sent-2411-426: However, selecting an appropriate polynomial that maintains the error detection capabilities of the standard \ac{crc while serving as a secure cryptographic function is non-trivial.}
elements.sent-2411-427: Identifying such polynomials is computationally intensive and time-consuming, rendering this approach less suitable for resource-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-428: To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel approach that modifies the \ac{iv used in the \ac{crc computation instead of employing a secret generator polynomial.}}
elements.sent-2411-429: By manipulating the IV, we avoid exhaustive searches for suitable polynomials and retain the standard generator, ensuring that the error detection capabilities remain intact.
elements.sent-2411-430: Furthermore, encrypting the \ac{crc with an \ac{otp provides unconditional security.}}
elements.sent-2411-431: This prevents attackers from deducing the original \ac{crc value of the \ac{iv used, thereby safeguarding the authentication mechanism against potential cryptographic attacks.}}
elements.sent-2411-432: In summary, our method addresses the key challenges associated with securing communications for legacy systems in resource-constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-433: We provide a lightweight, secure, and compatible authentication mechanism that preserves system performance and data throughput.
elements.sent-2411-434: This approach enables the effective integration of robust security measures into legacy systems without necessitating significant hardware modifications or compromising the operational characteristics that define these environments.
elements.sent-2411-435: In this paper, we identify key challenges that prevent existing authentication mechanisms designed to retrofit security in legacy protocols from being deployed in real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-436: To address these limitations, we developed ACRIC, a cryptographic \ac{crc computation methodology that ensures authentication and integrity protection for legacy protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-437: By leveraging the \ac{crc field, ACRIC preserves the original frame structure, maintaining full backward compatibility and data throughput for legacy protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-438: Additionally, ACRIC’s protocol-agnostic design allows for implementation across diverse application scenarios and communication models, including point-to-point and broadcast models.

elements.sent-2411-452: The increasing integration of modern Information Technology (IT) into Operational Technology (OT) and industrial systems is expanding the vulnerability surface of legacy infrastructures, which often rely on outdated protocols and resource-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-453: Recent security incidents in safety-critical industries exposed how the lack of proper message authentication enables attackers to inject malicious commands or alter system behavior, revealing fundamental security weaknesses in existing architectures.
elements.sent-2411-454: These shortcomings have thus prompted new regulations that emphasize the pressing need to strengthen cybersecurity, particularly in legacy systems.
elements.sent-2411-455: Message authentication is widely recognized as a fundamental security measure that enhances system resilience.
elements.sent-2411-456: However, its adoption in legacy industrial environments is limited due to practical challenges like backward compatibility, message format changes, and hardware replacement or upgrades costs.
elements.sent-2411-457: In this paper, we introduce ACRIC, a message authentication solution to secure legacy industrial communications explicitly tailored to overcome those challenges all at once.
elements.sent-2411-458: ACRIC uniquely leverages cryptographic computations applied to the Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) field - already present in most industrial communication protocols - ensuring robust message integrity protection and authentication without requiring additional hardware or modifications to existing message formats.
elements.sent-2411-459: ACRIC's backward compatibility and protocol-agnostic nature enable coexistence with non-secured devices, thus facilitating gradual security upgrades in legacy infrastructures.
elements.sent-2411-460: Formal security assessment and experimental evaluation on an industrial-grade testbed demonstrate that ACRIC provides robust security guarantees with minimal computational overhead (\(\sim 4 \mu s\)).
elements.sent-2411-461: These results underscore ACRIC's practicality, cost-effectiveness, and suitability for effective adoption in resource-constrained industrial environments.
elements.sent-2411-462: Industries such as power generation, oil and gas facilities, water treatment plants, and nuclear installations, which operate critical infrastructures through \ac{ot and \ac{ics are increasingly prioritizing cybersecurity to protect their systems from unauthorized access and potential harm~\cite{CybersecFor4.0 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-464: Recent high-profile incidents, including the 2010 Stuxnet attack on Iranian nuclear centrifuges~\cite{Stuxnet , the 2015 Ukraine power grid breach~\cite{UkraininianAttack , and the 2017 LogicLocker attack on a U.S.}}

elements.sent-2411-466: However, implementing modern cybersecurity in \ac{ot and \acp{ics poses practical challenges.}}

elements.sent-2411-480: We identify critical challenges in designing security solutions for legacy industrial protocols that currently limit the adoption of state-of-the-art solutions.

elements.section-2411-012: Securing Legacy Systems: Design Challenges and Goals
elements.sent-2411-506: This section discusses the primary challenges encountered when designing authentication mechanisms for legacy industrial communication protocols, which significantly limit the real-world adoption of proposed solutions in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-507: To identify key constraints and practical challenges, we conducted a comprehensive review of the literature and engaged in a close collaboration with CAREL~\footnote{CAREL is a global leader in control solutions for HVAC systems with over 50 years of experience and 15 production sites worldwide.}

elements.sent-2411-512: Significant research aimed to improve the security of legacy communication protocols, particularly within industrial and automotive networks.
elements.sent-2411-513: The rapid advancement of \ac{iiot and automated driving technologies has underscored the need for secure data exchanges in previously isolated, now interconnected systems \cite{CybersecurityICS , \cite{CybersecFor4.0 , \cite{CANSurvey .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-514: Although various security mechanisms such as digital signatures, \acp{mac , and encryption have been explored (\cite{CyberattacksCountermeasures , \cite{AnalysisSecurity , \cite{InvehicleAuth ), practical constraints limit their adoption in the real world, especially in resources-constrained environments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-515: By critically reviewing the available literature, we identify the following challenges.
elements.sent-2411-516: Simultaneous upgrades of all devices are impractical and costly, particularly in critical industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-517: Additionally, in \ac{ot systems, devices often rely on synchronized timing and regularly monitor the communication line, expecting messages of predefined lengths.}
elements.sent-2411-518: Introducing additional fields into message formats can disrupt this synchronization, leading to communication and system failures.
elements.sent-2411-519: Therefore, security solutions must maintain backward compatibility by preserving the original message frame structure, enabling secure and non-secure devices to coexist \cite{IndustrialPerspective , \cite{InvehicleAuth .}}
elements.sent-2411-521: Industrial communication protocols prioritize efficient data transmission due to limited bandwidth and the need for timely data exchange.
elements.sent-2411-522: Therefore, security measures should not reduce data throughput \cite{RetrofittingProtection .}
elements.sent-2411-523: Including authentication data within the payload reduces available space, negatively impacting throughput.
elements.sent-2411-525: Several proposed solutions introduce a gateway attached to legacy devices that encrypt/decrypt or parse the received messages, making the introduced security solution transparent for the legacy device.
elements.sent-2411-526: However, solutions requiring additional hardware or extensive infrastructure modifications are often prohibitively expensive \cite{IndustrialPerspective , \cite{CANSurvey .}}
elements.sent-2411-527: Large-scale device replacements are typically infeasible in industrial settings, making cost-effective deployment crucial for widespread adoption of security solutions.
elements.sent-2411-529: Real-time performance is essential for time-sensitive systems.
elements.sent-2411-530: Security mechanisms must be lightweight and computationally efficient to avoid compromising the real-time capabilities of these critical applications \cite{CANSurvey , \cite{SecurityChallenges .}}
elements.sent-2411-532: Existing solutions target specific communication protocols, requiring different implementations for each protocol and increasing deployment costs and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-533: An adaptable solution compatible with diverse protocols is therefore preferable \cite{RetrofittingProtection .}
elements.sent-2411-534: Legacy industrial protocols are vulnerable to various attacks, including message spoofing, modification, replay, \ac{mitm , and \ac{dos \cite{Fovino_et_al , \cite{attack_taxonomies , \cite{SecurityChallenges .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-535: Due to their critical nature, unlike traditional \ac{it systems, industrial \ac{ot systems generally prioritize availability and data integrity over confidentiality, as data changes or disruptions can significantly impact physical operations and safety~\cite{SecurityChallenges .}}}
elements.sent-2411-536: Confidentiality is typically less critical since industrial communications are often repetitive and predictable, allowing attackers to infer information without explicit decryption \cite{Castellanos-et-al , \cite{Wright-et-al .}}
elements.sent-2411-537: Given these considerations, the primary security objectives for legacy industrial systems include \cite{AnalysisSecurity , \cite{Fovino_et_al , \cite{SecurityChallenges : (i) Authentication, thanks to which we verify the legitimacy of incoming messages and communicating nodes; (ii) Integrity Protection, which prevents unauthorized modifications to messages preserving the accuracy and trustworthiness of the data; (iii) Replay Protection, which prevents the reuse of previously transmitted data to avoid unauthorized actions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-538: We present our operational goals to directly address previously identified challenges, thus ensuring an authentication mechanism to be practical and ready for real-world adoption:.
elements.sent-2411-540: The solution must allow the coexistence of secure and non-secure devices without changing the original frame format or adding new hardware.
elements.sent-2411-542: The authentication mechanism should preserve data throughput without requiring additional payload space.
elements.sent-2411-544: The solution must support the real-time requirements typical of industrial systems.
elements.sent-2411-546: The authentication mechanism should apply across various communication protocols; \ac{acric leverages the widespread presence of the \ac{crc field, making it adaptable to a broad range of legacy systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-547: By meeting these security and operational goals, \ac{acric effectively addresses the identified challenges, offering a practical and robust authentication solution explicitly tailored to legacy industrial communication environments.}
elements.sent-2411-548: In this section, we define the context and assumptions according to which we developed the \ac{acric framework.}
elements.sent-2411-549: Section~\ref{subsec:SystemModel outlines our system model, while Section~\ref{subsec:ThreatModel identifies adversaries capabilities and relevant attacks.}}
elements.sent-2411-550: We developed \ac{acric specifically to secure communications in legacy systems that involve older, resource-constrained devices with hard real-time demands for which replacing or significantly upgrading hardware is costly and impractical for these systems.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Thus, \ac{acric prioritizes backward compatibility and real-time guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Typical examples include \acp{ics and in-vehicle networks, widely recognized as representative legacy systems \cite{Castellanos-et-al , \cite{CANSurvey .}}}
elements.sent-2411-553: Besides, given their critical role in public safety and essential services, these domains urgently require practical and compatible security solutions.
elements.sent-2411-554: A widely adopted and standardized mechanism to ensure reliable data transmission in industrial communication protocols is the use of the \ac{crc field \cite{CRC3 , \cite{CRC2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-555: Since \ac{crc is already present in most legacy industrial protocols, we propose using it to provide security, preserving its original role for integrity check without altering the message format.}
elements.sent-2411-556: A key feature of our system model is the coexistence of secure and non-secure devices within the same network.
elements.sent-2411-557: In industrial environments, upgrading all devices simultaneously to new security standards is usually impractical due to logistical constraints, high costs, and the necessity for uninterrupted operation \cite{InvehicleNetworks , \cite{RetrofittingProtection .}}
elements.sent-2411-558: By allowing for phased security upgrades, we ensure the continuous functionality of critical infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-559: To maintain broad applicability, our model abstracts from specific network layouts and emphasizes essential functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-560: Consequently, it supports various communication protocols, providing authentication mechanisms suitable for both \ac{p2p and broadcast communication models (we treat multicast and unicast as special cases of these).}
elements.sent-2411-561: Examples of widely adopted protocols compatible with our approach include Modbus~\cite{modbus-application , CAN-bus~\cite{CAN , Profibus~\cite{Profibus , and DNP3~\cite{DNP3 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-562: This flexibility ensures that \ac{acric can be effectively applied across different network configurations, enhancing security while maintaining compatibility with existing infrastructures.}
elements.sent-2411-563: Our threat model builds on well-established frameworks in previous research (\cite{Bernieri-et-al , \cite{Katulic-et-al , \cite{CybersecCAN , \cite{CANSurvey ), and follows the widely accepted Dolev-Yao model~\cite{DV_model .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-564: Our model defines attacker capabilities and constraints consistently recognized in both networked and cyber-physical systems, ensuring alignment with standard assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-565: Specifically, we assume an attacker with complete access to network traffic but incapable of compromising hardware or internal cryptographic mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-566: Following Kerckhoff's principle~\cite{Kerckhoff_principle , the attacker is fully knowledgeable about the authentication protocol, relying solely on keys secrecy for protection.}
elements.sent-2411-567: Under these assumptions, potential attacker actions and consequences include:.
elements.sent-2411-568: Spoofing: The attacker sends messages appearing to originate from legitimate devices, leveraging compromised or guessed authentication parameters.
elements.sent-2411-569: This can result in the execution of unauthorized commands, leading to disruptions in operations and potential safety hazards.
elements.sent-2411-570: Tampering: The attacker intercepts and modifies data in transit without altering the authentication tag, possibly exploiting weaknesses in protocol implementation.
elements.sent-2411-571: Such modifications can cause control systems to malfunction or make incorrect operational decisions.
elements.sent-2411-572: Replay Attacks: By capturing legitimate messages and retransmitting them later, the attacker triggers unintended repeated actions.
elements.sent-2411-573: This can lead to undesired redundancy, resource exhaustion, or unauthorized activation of system processes.
elements.sent-2411-574: Brute-force Attacks: The attacker systematically tests authentication values until finding a valid one.
elements.sent-2411-575: Success could grant unauthorized access or control, compromising system integrity.
elements.sent-2411-576: We explicitly exclude threats such as \ac{dos , fingerprinting, and side-channel attacks, as these require additional defensive strategies, like network monitoring and anomaly detection, which go beyond cryptographic authentication alone~\cite{CANSurvey .}}
elements.sent-2411-577: In Section \ref{subsec:Overview we first give an overview of \ac{acric 's principles and functioning, while in Section \ref{subsec:TechnicalOperation we present its technical details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-578: Then, in Section \ref{sec:ManageCrypto we present recommendations for managing the cryptographic material.}
elements.sent-2411-579: Overview Communication protocols typically use a \acrfull{crc field to detect transmission errors, calculated using a standardized \acrfull{iv , a generator polynomial and message content~\cite{CRC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-580: This standard \ac{crc mechanism lacks any security properties, particularly authentication and integrity protection.}
elements.sent-2411-581: Hence, an attacker capable of modifying message payloads can easily recalculate and replace the \ac{crc , thereby evading detection.}
elements.sent-2411-582: The core innovation of \ac{crc lies in the introduction of two complementary enhancements:.}
elements.sent-2411-583: A secret \ac{iv , shared only among authorized entities, is used for \ac{crc computations, binding message authenticity to the secrecy of this \ac{iv .}}}
elements.sent-2411-584: An OTP encryption, using single-use secret keys derived from a hash chain, protects the secrecy of the \ac{crc and, consequently, prevents attackers from deducing the used \ac{iv .}}
elements.sent-2411-585: These enhancements enable \ac{acric to significantly strengthen legacy communication protocols, ensuring robust authentication and integrity while maintaining full compatibility with legacy systems and interoperability between secured and unsecured devices.}
elements.sent-2411-586: Figure~\ref{fig:Authentication gives a graphical representation of the acric computation procedure using the secret \ac{iv and hash values \(h_i\).}}

The secret \ac{iv} and message \(M\) are inputs to the standard \ac{crc} algorithm \(F_{CRC}\left(\cdot, \cdot\right)\). The resulting \ac{crc} is \ac{otp} encrypted (XOR-ed) with a hash value to protect the \ac{iv}'s confidentiality.} \label{fig:Authentication}
elements.sent-2411-587: Operational Procedure The specific implementation for system initialization and distribution of cryptographic material is out of the scope of this work as it shall be designed according to the specific network topology, constraints, and manufacturer convenience.
elements.sent-2411-588: Consequently, we assume that upon successful system initialization, each pair of nodes shares the following cryptographic parameters:.
elements.sent-2411-590: ACRIC computation begins by following the standard \ac{crc calculation procedure, modified to use a secret \ac{iv instead of a standardized one.}}
elements.sent-2411-591: For an \(n\)-byte \ac{crc , we define the secret \ac{iv using the session key \(K_s = \left[k^s_0 || k^s_1 || \cdots || k^s_N\right]\), with each \(k^s_i\) as an 8-bit binary sequence: \(InitVec = \left[k^s_0 || \cdots || k^s_n\right]\).}}
elements.sent-2411-592: A matching \ac{crc thus confirms the authenticity and integrity of the message.}
elements.sent-2411-593: However, using only a secret \ac{iv is insufficient for security, as attackers could infer it through an exhaustive analysis of message payloads and \ac{crc values.}}
elements.sent-2411-595: To overcome the limitation described above, \ac{acric secures the computed \ac{crc through \ac{otp encryption.}}}
elements.sent-2411-596: Specifically, the computed \ac{crc value is XOR-encrypted using a single-use secret key derived from the hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\): \begin{equation}\label{eq:authentication} ACRIC_i = CRC_i \oplus h_i = F_{CRC}(InitVec, M_i) \oplus h_i,\end{equation} where \(F_{CRC (\cdot, \cdot)\) is the standard \ac{crc calculation function, \(M_i\) is the \(i\)-th message, and \(h_i\) is the \(i\)-th hash value in the chain.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:authentication} ACRIC_i = CRC_i \oplus h_i = F_{CRC}(InitVec, M_i) \oplus h_i,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-597: The hash chain \(\mathcal{H}\) provides a series of single-use encryption keys, ensuring the \ac{otp 's perfect secrecy property and thus fully protecting \ac{iv confidentiality \cite{OTP .}}}
elements.sent-2411-598: Effective management of cryptographic material is essential when securing legacy industrial systems, especially given their constraints regarding memory and computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-599: The management of cryptographic material directly impacts the effectiveness of the security measure, the complexity of the deployment, and the operational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-600: In the following, we outline practical considerations and offer clear recommendations for managing cryptographic parameters, focusing on system initialization procedures, key management strategies, and hash chain handling.
elements.sent-2411-601: Table \ref{tab:CryptoManagement summarizes our discussion.. System Initialization.}
elements.sent-2411-602: Minimizing manual intervention during deployment and maintenance is crucial in large-scale industrial environments due to the inherent complexity and high risk of human error.
elements.sent-2411-603: Although local initialization - where session keys are derived locally without device interactions - reduces runtime overhead, it demands precise manual configuration of cryptographic parameters.
elements.sent-2411-604: Such manual setups significantly increase the likelihood of errors, especially in large-scale environments, potentially compromising network security and causing initialization failures.
elements.sent-2411-605: To simplify and secure the system setup, we then recommend using an interactive initialization strategy.
elements.sent-2411-606: Specifically, storing only a master key \(K_m\) shared across all network nodes allows devices to dynamically generate session keys, simplifying the system setup although introducing some overhead.
elements.sent-2411-607: Employing an authenticated Diffie-Hellman key exchange procedure is particularly beneficial, as it provides a secure, flexible, and scalable solution applicable to both \ac{p2p and broadcast scenarios.. Key Management.}
elements.sent-2411-608: The choice of the key management strategy significantly influences the security and operational efficiency of \ac{acric in different communication models.}
elements.sent-2411-609: A Pair-based Key Management is ideal for \ac{p2p communications, assigning unique session keys to each pair of communicating devices.}
elements.sent-2411-610: Compared to device-based management, this method simplifies key distribution, parameters synchronization, and reduces storage overhead.
elements.sent-2411-611: Similarly, a Group-based Key Management is recommended for broadcast communication scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-612: In this approach, devices within logical groups share common cryptographic keys.
elements.sent-2411-613: Compared to device-based key management - which incurs in high storage overhead and security risks - the group-based method substantially reduces memory requirements and simplifies synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-614: This approach is particularly advantageous in large systems, as exemplified by its extensive consideration as a feasible key management strategy to introduce authentication within the CAN-bus protocol~\cite{CANSurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-615: However, a careful implementation of the group-key method is crucial to avoid security vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-616: Group memberships must be strictly managed, assigning only the minimum necessary permissions aligned to their roles~\cite{CANSurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-617: Such rigorous management effectively minimizes unauthorized access and mitigates masquerade attack risks.
elements.sent-2411-618: The practical and efficient handling of hash chains, which supply the \ac{otp encryption keys, involves three core components: consumption, storage, and computation.}
elements.sent-2411-619: Regarding Hash Chain Consumption, we recommend a forward-consumption strategy, sequentially using hash values from root to tail.
elements.sent-2411-620: This approach minimizes memory demands compared to a backward-consumption strategy as it does not require pre-computing the entire hash chain.
elements.sent-2411-621: We highlight that it requires embedding a secret key in the hash function to maintain forward secrecy.
elements.sent-2411-622: For Hash Chain Storage, as well as for key management, we advise for a pair-based (for P2P) or group-based (for broadcast) solution over entity-based storage.
elements.sent-2411-623: These methods simplify synchronization and significantly reduce memory usage, aligning with requirements of resource-constrained environments and legacy devices.
elements.sent-2411-624: Finally, for Hash Chain Computation we suggest employing a hybrid computation approach, i.e., precomputing part of the hash chain offline and generating the remainder dynamically, This allows to effectively balance between computational load and memory usage, ensuring both security and operational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-625: Another practical aspect to consider is whether the hash value should be updated after every message transmission or upon successful message reception.
elements.sent-2411-626: Industrial protocols typically interpret rejected messages as transmission failures, prompting retransmissions a limited number of times before suspending the transmitting device if failures persist \cite{CAN , \cite{modbus-application .}}
elements.sent-2411-627: In such scenarios, reusing the same hash value for retransmissions generally poses minimal risk.
elements.sent-2411-628: However, attackers may change the ACRIC value with each retransmission, thus reducing the pool of valid authentication candidates.
elements.sent-2411-629: While device suspension after repeated transmission failures can deter brute-force attacks, updating the hash value for every retransmission significantly enhances security in critical applications where even a single malicious message could be highly detrimental.
elements.sent-2411-630: This prevents attackers from systematically narrowing down the set of potential valid authentication tags.
elements.sent-2411-631: Nonetheless, this solution accelerates hash chain consumption and increases computational overhead due to frequent hash chain refreshes, demanding careful consideration aligned with the application's criticality and specific operational constraints.

elements.sent-2411-685: In this paper, we addressed the problem of retrofitting security within legacy industrial and \ac{ot systems, identifying practical challenges hindering the real-world adoption of authentication solutions proposed in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-686: These include enabling interoperability between secure and non-secure devices, preserving message format and data throughput, minimizing deployment costs and overhead, and ensuring compatibility with mixed systems.
elements.sent-2411-687: To overcome all these limitations at once, we designed \acrfull{acric , an authentication mechanism that leverages cryptographic computation of the existing \acrfull{crc field, ensuring message authentication and integrity protection.}}
elements.sent-2411-688: Compared to existing solutions, \ac{acric preserves the original frame structure and does not require additional hardware, ensuring full backward compatibility and throughput for legacy protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-689: Moreover, its protocol-agnostic design enables seamless integration into diverse industrial scenarios, supporting both point-to-point and broadcast communications.
elements.sent-2411-690: Formal analysis and experimental validation with commercial devices demonstrate \ac{acric ’s robust security and minimal computational overhead (\(\sim 4 \mu s\)), confirming its practicality for securing legacy industrial applications.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04.}}
